note: SNG-065 — the shape a quest must have. Authored by Aevi 2026-07-11 on Erik's direction: 'I want the quests to be meaningful and have some concrete structure.'
theRule: IF YOU CANNOT NAME THE COST OF IGNORING IT, IT IS NOT A QUEST. It is an errand. Errands are fine; they are not quests and must not be logged as such.

required.stakes: What is at risk, and WHO PAYS if the player walks away. Mandatory. This is the field that separates a quest from a fetch.
required.axis: The axis this quest lives ON. A quest is a TENSION BETWEEN TWO POLES — that is what makes it a dilemma instead of a chore. If you cannot name the axis, the quest has no spine.
required.traditions: Which peoples' crafts open routes through it. Feeds the multi-route requirement.
required.stages: 2-5. Each: objective (one sentence, player-statable) + condition (ENGINE-TESTABLE: place reached / person spoken to / thing obtained / truth learned / roll passed) + change (what shifts in the world when this stage lands). NO vague 'investigate further'.
required.routes: Force, guile, care, reason, craft, concealment, truth, endurance. A quest with ONE solution is a lock, not a story. Routes fan across the great circle so a player's domains change how they approach it.
required.outcomes: 2-4 BRANCHED endings. NOT success/fail — WHICH success. Each names its own consequences.
required.consequences: MANDATORY, per outcome. At least one DURABLE change: a codex fact, an NPC's life/standing/death, a people's disposition toward you, a location's state, or a world-event that PROPAGATES (dated on the shared clock, surfacing later via the away-digest). A quest that changes nothing durable is not allowed to be a quest.

optional.legend: The SNG-042 figure this touches, if any.
optional.tier: riffraff / regional / epic — scales with the player's arc.

designLaws: The best quests have no clean exit. Someone is worse off whatever you choose; the quest is about WHO, and that is the player's to answer for.
Never punish the route. Force, guile, and care are all legitimate; they lead to DIFFERENT outcomes, not better and worse ones.
The consequence must be findable later. If the player cannot go back and SEE what they did, it did not happen.
A quest should make at least one person permanently different — including, sometimes, the player.
